title: The Second Fruit Is Joy
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 10-6-24 - The Second Fruit is Joy - Nehemiah 8_8-12 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Joy of the Lord: Three Interpretations
subpoints: The joy the Lord has in himself — his own happiness within the Trinity
The joy the Lord gives to his people — resulting from the indwelling of the Holy Spirit
The joy we have in the Lord — the object of our joy is God himself
All three combine for a full understanding of biblical joy

outline.title: Six Truths About the Joy of the Lord
subpoints: 1. Joy is not the joy of circumstances or temperament — it is distinctly the joy of the Lord
2. Joy comes out of true repentance — there is an irony: the believer both mourns over sin and rejoices in the Lord (2 Cor. 6:10)
3. Joy is the joy of God's forgiveness and covenant love — blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven (Ps. 32)
4. Joy comes from properly understanding God's Word — the people rejoiced because they understood what was declared to them (Neh. 8:12)
5. Joy is joy in God himself — he is the treasure, not merely the blessings he gives
6. Joy is not at odds with holiness — it is the fruit of the Holy Spirit; holy joy and holiness always go together

outline.title: Lack of Joy Is Always Idolatrous
subpoints: If being like Jesus is not good enough for us, we lack joy because we are desiring something more than God
This is hopeful: all it takes is repentance and turning to the Lord
The world says choose between joy and obedience to God — but this is Satan's lie; joy comes from following God

outline.title: Joy Is Personal and Corporate
subpoints: The joy of the Lord is to be experienced both personally and corporately
The Lord's Day gathering is a primary expression of corporate joy
Treating the Lord's Day as unimportant is saying you don't need the strength that comes from the joy of the Lord
The joy of the Lord is always available to all who trust in Jesus for salvation

summary: In this fourth sermon of the Fruit of the Spirit series, Pastor Tito Lyro turns to Nehemiah 8 to illuminate the second fruit of the Spirit: joy. Using the scene of Ezra reading the Law while the people weep and then are commanded to rejoice because "the joy of the Lord is your strength," he unpacks three ways to understand the expression — joy the Lord has in himself, joy the Lord gives, and joy we have in the Lord — and insists all three combine for a full understanding of biblical joy. He then develops six truths about this joy: it is not rooted in circumstances or temperament; it arises from true repentance; it flows from the joy of God's forgiveness and covenant love; it comes from properly understanding God's Word; it is ultimately joy in God himself rather than his blessings; and it is not at odds with holiness but is the fruit of the Holy Spirit. Along the way he makes the provocative claim that lack of joy is always idolatrous — we lack joy when we are desiring something more than God himself. The sermon is both sobering and deeply encouraging, insisting that inexpressible joy is not the privilege of the "super-Christian" but the birthright of every believer in Jesus Christ, and that it is always available through repentance and trust in the Lord.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading

If you are able to remain standing, we are turning to the book of Nehemiah, chapter 8. We are going to read verses 1 through 12.

*[Scripture reading: Nehemiah 8:1-12]*

This is the word of your God. Let us pray together. Father, we pray that you would speak to us through the preaching of your word. Father, help me to be faithful to you. We pray that you take me out of your way and that your Spirit would cause your people to see Jesus. In his name, Amen. Please be seated.

## The Series: Arriving at Joy

We continue in our series on the fruit of the Spirit. In Galatians 5:22-23, the Holy Spirit through the apostle Paul says: *The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control, against such there is no law.*

Today we arrive at the second one listed in verse 22: the fruit of the Spirit is love and then **joy**. The joy of the Lord is our strength — that is what Nehemiah, Ezra, and the Levites tell the people of God in Nehemiah 8:10.

I don't know anyone who would say, "You know, I just want to be miserable." Most of us want joy, but we often struggle to find it or it seems fleeting. The Scriptures teach that a joyful life is not only possible but also likely for those who follow Jesus Christ. The apostle Peter writes in 1 Peter 1:8: *Though now you do not see him, yet believing, you rejoice with joy inexpressible and full of glory.* Peter states it as a present fact: you, Christian, have joy inexpressible. How do we reconcile that with our experience?

Remember: these nine items are one single fruit of the Spirit. Every Christian upon being born again exhibits all nine fruits, at different intensities. Joy is a fruit of the Spirit, given to every believer. Our ancient brothers and sisters in Nehemiah 8 can help us answer the question of how to experience it.

## The Joy of the Lord: Three Interpretations

At the end of verse 10 of Nehemiah 8, Ezra, Nehemiah, and the Levites together say: *Do not sorrow, for the joy of the Lord is your strength.* There are at least three ways to understand the expression "the joy of the Lord."

**First:** The joy the Lord has in himself — his own joy in his existence, in his fellowship within the Trinity. In this case, our strength lies in the fact that God is happy in himself. Because we have a happy God, we are a happy people.

**Second:** The joy the Lord gives to his people — joy that belongs to him and that he gives. This is the result of the indwelling of the Holy Spirit. Our strength is the joy of knowing we have been saved and nothing can change that.

**Third:** The joy we have in the Lord himself — he is the object of our joy. Our strength is the identity of God as revealed in the Bible — knowing who he is.

I think the Scriptures combine all three when they speak about joy. These three interpretations give us a full-orbed understanding of joy.

## Six Truths About the Joy of the Lord

### 1. The Joy of the Lord Is Not the Joy of Circumstances or Temperament

This is obvious from the fact that it is distinctly called *the joy of the Lord* — not the joy of your circumstances or the joy of who you are. People in the world can have some sort of joy when something good happens to them. But the joy of the Lord is something that only those who know the Lord can enjoy *in spite of* circumstances.

David knew this. In Psalm 13, he cries out *How long, Lord, will you forget me forever?* — an ongoing sorrow. But at the end of this short Psalm he states by faith, contrary to his circumstances: *My heart shall rejoice in your salvation. I will sing to the Lord because He has dealt bountifully with me.* Paul, writing Philippians while in prison, with fellow Christians badmouthing him and false teachers attacking his churches, commands the believers to *rejoice in the Lord always.* Paul was in complete tune with reality — the reality that God is his God through Jesus Christ, and that all things were working together for his good as he was conformed to the image of Jesus Christ. Brothers and sisters, that is your reality.

### 2. The Joy of the Lord Comes Out of True Repentance

In verses 9-12, the people are weeping as they hear the Word of God. These are tears of repentance — Chapter 9, an entire chapter, is a prayer of repentance. Yet they are commanded to rejoice. There is an irony in Scripture: the believer both mourns over sin and rejoices in the Lord. Paul puts that together in 2 Corinthians 6:10: *sorrowful yet always rejoicing.* Both happen simultaneously.

Some people are by nature more introspective and spend too much time looking at their sins. Robert Murray McCheyne says: *Learn much of the Lord Jesus. For every look at yourself, take ten looks at Christ.* But the greater problem for most Christians is actually being too flippant about sin, shrugging it off by saying, "We're under grace." If my sin put the sinless Son of God on the cross, I cannot take it lightly. The proper balance: take sin seriously and truly repent of it; then put our focus back on Christ and our position in him by grace. We should visit repentance as often as needed but dwell in the joy of God's grace.

### 3. The Joy of the Lord Is the Joy of God's Forgiveness and Covenant Love

All the great feasts of Israel, including the Feast of Tabernacles being celebrated here, were reminders of God's abundant mercy to his chosen people in spite of their sin and failure. In Psalm 32:1-2, David celebrates: *Blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered. Blessed is the man to whom the Lord does not impute iniquity.* Joy comes from forgiven sins. The Psalm ends: *Be glad in the Lord and rejoice, you righteous; and shout for joy, all you upright in heart* — because your sins have been forgiven in Jesus Christ.

### 4. Joy Comes from Properly Understanding God's Word

In verse 12: *All the people went their way to eat and drink and send portions and rejoice greatly, because they understood the words that were declared to them.* God's Word makes known to us his exceedingly great and precious promises (2 Pet. 1:4). When you go through deep trials, Satan tempts you to think that God does not care for you and it is easy to quit reading your Bible. But especially at such times you need to be devouring your Bible, because the joy of the Lord comes from understanding his Word. The Bible reassures us of God's love and grace. It tells us of other believers who endured incredibly difficult trials. It gives us needed perspective: what you are going through is hard and difficult, but compared to eternal Christ it is light and momentary — not because God is downplaying your trial, but because he is elevating the weight of his presence in eternity.

### 5. The Joy of the Lord Is Joy in God Himself

By nature we tend to find joy in things other than God himself — our health, food, relationships, possessions. As long as we thankfully acknowledge these as gifts from God's hand, that is not a problem. But we should be growing to know God himself as our treasure. He is the treasure, not the blessings that come from him. Remember the parable of the treasure hidden in the field: when the man knows there is a treasure, he divests himself of everything else to have it, because nothing in life compares to that.

We should find such joy in the very being of God that even if everything were stripped away, we would rejoice in him alone. Psalm 16:11: *In your presence is fullness of joy.* Zephaniah 3:17: *The Lord your God in your midst, the Mighty One, will save. He will rejoice over you with gladness. He will quiet you with his love. He will rejoice over you with singing.* That is the God who is our strength.

Spend a week meditating on who God is — not on what he has done for you, but on his attributes. Stephen Charnock's *Attributes of God* or A.W. Pink's *Attributes of God* are excellent for this. Consider his righteousness, his justice, his holiness, his sovereignty, his love. The more you know God through Jesus Christ, the greater your joy will be.

### 6. The Joy of the Lord Is Not at Odds with Holiness

Three times in this passage (verses 9, 10, 11) the leaders tell the people not to weep but to rejoice, because this is a holy day of the Lord. It is the holiness of what is happening because of God that should lead them to rejoice. Holiness and joy are not enemies — they are intimately linked. Joy is the fruit of *whom*? The Holy Spirit. The world parades happy people who are immoral, as if happiness and immorality go together. But happiness is promised by following God in his Word, not by rejecting it.

Lack of joy is **always idolatrous.** When we lack joy, it is because we are desiring something more than God as revealed to us in Jesus Christ. This is actually hopeful news: all it takes is repentance and turning to the Lord.

## Joy Is Personal and Corporate

The joy of the Lord is to be experienced both personally and corporately. The corporate expression of joy is represented as we gather together on the Lord's Day. When we decide that the Lord's Day is not important and go do something else, we are saying we don't need the corporate expression of the joy of the Lord — which means we don't need the strength that comes from it. And then we wonder why life is hard and there is sadness and struggle.

But brothers and sisters, the joy of the Lord is always available to all of you who trust in Jesus for your salvation. It is there. It is not for the super-Christian — it is for you who believe. It is a matter of choice. The joy of the Lord is your strength. Are you walking in that strength?

Father in heaven, thank you that you are a good God who is our joy, and because of that you are also our strength. Enable us to rejoice in you. We pray that you help us to repent of our many idolatrous desires and that the Spirit would have victory over the flesh in us that we might rejoice in you with joy inexpressible. In Jesus' name, Amen.
